plot_plan: I'll create visualizations comparing the three override strategies (confidence-gated, always_accept, never_accept) across different interaction modes (supportive, challenging, adaptive). The plots will show how appropriate reliance, decision accuracy, and agreement rates evolve during training and compare final performance metrics. I'll generate line plots showing metric progression over epochs for each mode, bar charts comparing final results, and a heatmap visualizing appropriate reliance scores across all conditions.

plot_analyses.analysis: Agreement-rate learning curves under three override strategies (confidence-gated, always-accept, never-accept) across modes show distinct behavioral regimes. Supportive: all strategies converge high (~0.92–0.94) by ~60–80 epochs; always-accept is marginally highest at convergence, confidence-gated slightly lower, never-accept slightly lower still. Challenging: always-accept stays at 1.0 agreement throughout training (degenerate full compliance), confidence-gated starts ~1.0 then declines slightly to ~0.98 by late epochs, and never-accept rises from ~0.78 to ~0.93–0.95 then dips slightly—indicating the override policy strongly dominates agreement behavior in challenging interactions. Adaptive: strategies converge tightly (~0.92–0.94); always-accept is higher early, but late-epoch differences are minimal, suggesting the adaptive interaction style reduces sensitivity of agreement to override choice compared with challenging mode.

Decision-accuracy learning curves under the same override strategies. Supportive: all strategies improve from ~0.75–0.77 to ~0.94–0.96; confidence-gated and always-accept end essentially tied (~0.953), while never-accept ends slightly lower (~0.938), implying some benefit from allowing acceptance/override rather than rigid rejection. Challenging: always-accept is flat at ~0.908 across epochs (no learning benefit / possibly constrained), while never-accept climbs strongly from ~0.74 to ~0.94 and confidence-gated climbs from ~0.91 to ~0.93—here, always-accept harms accuracy relative to the other two, consistent with overreliance/compliance when the challenging AI may be wrong or adversarially framed. Adaptive: all strategies rise from ~0.70–0.76 to ~0.95; final accuracies are extremely close (0.950–0.953), indicating override strategy matters much more for reliance calibration than raw accuracy in adaptive mode.

Final-metrics bar-grid (Appropriate Reliance, Decision Accuracy, Agreement Rate) summarizes the ablation. Appropriate Reliance: Supportive—confidence-gated best (0.827) > always-accept (0.811) > never-accept (0.786). Challenging—never-accept best (0.772) >> confidence-gated (0.627) > always-accept (0.500). Adaptive—never-accept best (0.818) ≈ confidence-gated (0.811) > always-accept (0.793). Decision Accuracy: Supportive—confidence-gated and always-accept tie (0.953) > never-accept (0.938). Challenging—never-accept (0.942) > confidence-gated (0.932) > always-accept (0.908). Adaptive—all ~0.95 (0.953 gated, 0.950 always, 0.952 never). Agreement Rate: Supportive—always-accept slightly highest (0.928) vs gated (0.922) vs never (0.920). Challenging—always-accept is 1.000, gated 0.977, never 0.930. Adaptive—always-accept 0.932, gated 0.928, never 0.923. Key implication: agreement is not a proxy for appropriate reliance; in challenging mode, maximal agreement coincides with worst appropriate reliance.

Decision-accuracy across override strategies (duplicate of the earlier accuracy curves) reinforces that challenging+always-accept is uniquely problematic: it plateaus at ~0.908 while other strategies continue improving. In supportive/adaptive, differences are small at convergence, suggesting accuracy is robust to override choice except in the challenging persona where compliance suppresses corrective behavior.

Final metrics (duplicate visualization of the bar-grid) again highlights the central interaction: the optimal override strategy is mode-dependent. Supportive benefits from confidence gating (best reliance without sacrificing accuracy). Challenging benefits from never-accept (best reliance and accuracy), suggesting that in a challenging persona, users/system should resist accepting AI outputs automatically; gating may still allow too much acceptance under miscalibrated confidence. Adaptive shows near-tie between never-accept and confidence-gated, hinting that adaptation already mitigates some risks, but always-accept remains consistently worse for reliance.

Appropriate-reliance learning curves show how reliance calibration evolves. Supportive: confidence-gated steadily rises to ~0.83; always-accept catches up late to ~0.81; never-accept rises to ~0.79 then slightly declines/plateaus—suggesting rigid rejection prevents optimal reliance calibration. Challenging: always-accept is flat at 0.50 (chance-level reliance), confidence-gated slowly improves from ~0.50 to ~0.63 late, while never-accept improves monotonically to ~0.77—strong evidence that acceptance in challenging mode is actively harmful to appropriate reliance. Adaptive: never-accept leads early and remains highest (~0.82); confidence-gated improves steadily to ~0.81; always-accept lags early, briefly catches up around ~70 epochs, then ends lower (~0.79). The learning dynamics suggest switching costs/instability for always-accept in adaptive mode.

Appropriate-reliance heatmap compactly summarizes the same table: best cells are Supportive+confidence-gated (0.827) and Adaptive+never-accept (0.818), while the worst is Challenging+always-accept (0.500). The heatmap emphasizes that the challenging persona’s reliance is highly sensitive to override policy, whereas supportive/adaptive are comparatively stable.

Agreement-rate across override strategies (duplicate of the agreement curves) reiterates that challenging+always-accept yields perfect agreement (1.0) but does not translate to better outcomes; it likely indicates overcompliance. In supportive/adaptive, agreement converges similarly across strategies, so agreement is not discriminative there either.

Appropriate-reliance scores heatmap (duplicate) again underscores mode × override interaction: in challenging mode, moving from always-accept to never-accept yields +0.272 appropriate reliance (0.500→0.772), far larger than any other delta, making this the dominant effect in this ablation.

Appropriate-reliance curves (duplicate) confirm that in challenging mode, confidence-gating only partially mitigates the always-accept failure mode; never-accept is the only strategy that achieves strong and steadily improving reliance. In supportive mode, confidence-gating provides a modest but consistent advantage over always-accept and never-accept at convergence.

vlm_feedback_summary: Plots evaluate a confidence-based override ablation (confidence-gated vs always-accept vs never-accept) across supportive, challenging, and adaptive interaction modes. Main finding is a strong mode×override interaction: supportive performs best with confidence gating (highest appropriate reliance with no accuracy loss), challenging performs best with never-accept and catastrophically fails with always-accept (perfect agreement but worst reliance and lower accuracy), and adaptive is relatively robust with never-accept slightly best and confidence-gated close behind. Agreement rate is shown to be a misleading proxy for appropriate reliance, especially in challenging mode.

I will modify the baseline code to remove the separate AI model pre-training phase and instead train both the human decision model and AI assistant model jointly from scratch during each interaction mode. The AI model will start untrained and evolve alongside the human model during the 100 training epochs. I'll track both AI model performance and human-AI collaboration metrics to observe how co-evolution affects appropriate reliance compared to the baseline where AI starts pre-trained. The experiment will compare all three interaction modes (supportive, challenging, adaptive) under this joint training paradigm.

import os
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from collections import defaultdict

# Setup
working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")
os.makedirs(working_dir, exist_ok=True)

device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
print(f"Using device: {device}")

# Set random seeds
np.random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# Initialize experiment data structure
experiment_data = {
    "supportive": {
        "metrics": {
            "appropriate_reliance": [],
            "decision_accuracy": [],
            "agreement_rate": [],
            "ai_accuracy": [],
        },
        "losses": {
            "human": [],
            "ai": [],
        },
        "cognitive_loads": [],
        "epochs": [],
    },
    "challenging": {
        "metrics": {
            "appropriate_reliance": [],
            "decision_accuracy": [],
            "agreement_rate": [],
            "ai_accuracy": [],
        },
        "losses": {
            "human": [],
            "ai": [],
        },
        "cognitive_loads": [],
        "epochs": [],
    },
    "adaptive": {
        "metrics": {
            "appropriate_reliance": [],
            "decision_accuracy": [],
            "agreement_rate": [],
            "ai_accuracy": [],
        },
        "losses": {
            "human": [],
            "ai": [],
        },
        "cognitive_loads": [],
        "epochs": [],
    },
}


# Generate synthetic dataset for decision-making tasks
def generate_decision_task_data(n_samples=2000, n_features=20):
    """
    Generate synthetic data for collaborative decision-making
    Features represent task characteristics (e.g., medical symptoms, financial indicators)
    """
    X = np.random.randn(n_samples, n_features).astype(np.float32)
    # True labels (ground truth)
    weights = np.random.randn(n_features)
    logits = X @ weights + np.random.randn(n_samples) * 0.5
    y_true = (logits > 0).astype(np.float32)

    # Task complexity (affects cognitive load)
    task_complexity = np.abs(logits) / (np.abs(logits).max())  # normalized difficulty
    task_complexity = 1 - task_complexity  # higher = more difficult

    return X, y_true, task_complexity


# Human decision model (influenced by cognitive load)
class HumanDecisionModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim=20, hidden_dim=64):
        super(HumanDecisionModel, self).__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc3 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(0.3)

    def forward(self, x, cognitive_load=None):
        x = torch.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = self.dropout(x)
        x = torch.relu(self.fc2(x))

        # If under high cognitive load, add noise to simulate degraded performance
        if cognitive_load is not None and self.training:
            noise = torch.randn_like(x) * cognitive_load.unsqueeze(1) * 0.5
            x = x + noise

        x = self.fc3(x)
        return x


# AI assistant model
class AIAssistantModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim=20, hidden_dim=64):
        super(AIAssistantModel, self).__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc3 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = torch.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = torch.relu(self.fc2(x))
        x = self.fc3(x)
        return x


# Generate data
print("Generating synthetic decision-making data...")
X, y_true, task_complexity = generate_decision_task_data(n_samples=2000, n_features=20)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test, complexity_train, complexity_test = train_test_split(
    X, y_true, task_complexity, test_size=0.3, random_state=42
)

# Convert to tensors
X_train_t = torch.FloatTensor(X_train).to(device)
y_train_t = torch.FloatTensor(y_train).to(device)
complexity_train_t = torch.FloatTensor(complexity_train).to(device)
X_test_t = torch.FloatTensor(X_test).to(device)
y_test_t = torch.FloatTensor(y_test).to(device)
complexity_test_t = torch.FloatTensor(complexity_test).to(device)

print("\nABLATION STUDY: Joint Training (No AI Pre-training)")
print("AI model will be trained jointly with human model from scratch")


# Function to simulate human-AI interaction
def simulate_interaction(
    human_model,
    ai_model,
    X,
    y_true,
    cognitive_load,
    interaction_mode,
    adaptive_threshold=0.6,
):
    """
    Simulate human-AI collaborative decision making
    interaction_mode: 'supportive', 'challenging', or 'adaptive'
    """
    human_model.eval()
    ai_model.eval()

    with torch.no_grad():
        # Get initial human decision
        human_logits = human_model(X, cognitive_load).squeeze()
        human_initial = torch.sigmoid(human_logits) > 0.5
        human_confidence = torch.abs(torch.sigmoid(human_logits) - 0.5) * 2  # 0 to 1

        # Get AI recommendation
        ai_logits = ai_model(X).squeeze()
        ai_recommendation = torch.sigmoid(ai_logits) > 0.5
        ai_correct = ai_recommendation == y_true

        # Determine AI behavior based on mode
        if interaction_mode == "supportive":
            # AI always agrees/supports human
            ai_challenges = torch.zeros_like(human_initial, dtype=torch.bool)
        elif interaction_mode == "challenging":
            # AI always challenges when disagrees
            ai_challenges = ai_recommendation != human_initial
        else:  # adaptive
            # AI challenges only when cognitive load is low
            high_cognitive_load = cognitive_load > adaptive_threshold
            ai_challenges = (ai_recommendation != human_initial) & (
                ~high_cognitive_load
            )

        # Human final decision (may change if AI challenges and human has low confidence)
        change_decision = ai_challenges & (human_confidence < 0.6)
        human_final = human_initial.clone()
        human_final[change_decision] = ai_recommendation[change_decision]

        # Calculate metrics
        agreement_with_ai = (human_final == ai_recommendation).float()

        # Appropriate reliance score
        agreement_when_ai_correct = (
            agreement_with_ai[ai_correct].mean()
            if ai_correct.sum() > 0
            else torch.tensor(0.0)
        )
        disagreement_when_ai_incorrect = (
            (1 - agreement_with_ai[~ai_correct]).mean()
            if (~ai_correct).sum() > 0
            else torch.tensor(0.0)
        )
        appropriate_reliance = (
            agreement_when_ai_correct + disagreement_when_ai_incorrect
        ) / 2

        # Decision accuracy
        decision_accuracy = (human_final == y_true).float().mean()

        # AI accuracy
        ai_accuracy = (ai_recommendation == y_true).float().mean()

        # Overall agreement rate
        agreement_rate = agreement_with_ai.mean()

    return {
        "appropriate_reliance": appropriate_reliance.item(),
        "decision_accuracy": decision_accuracy.item(),
        "agreement_rate": agreement_rate.item(),
        "ai_accuracy": ai_accuracy.item(),
        "human_final": human_final,
        "ai_recommendation": ai_recommendation,
    }


# Train human decision models and AI models jointly
print("\n" + "=" * 80)
print("Joint Training: Human and AI Models Training Together")
print("=" * 80)

modes = ["supportive", "challenging", "adaptive"]
n_epochs = 100

for mode in modes:
    print(f"\n{'='*80}")
    print(f"Joint training with {mode.upper()} AI interaction mode")
    print(f"{'='*80}")

    # Create new human model AND AI model for this condition (no pre-training)
    human_model = HumanDecisionModel(input_dim=20, hidden_dim=64).to(device)
    ai_model = AIAssistantModel(input_dim=20, hidden_dim=64).to(device)

    human_optimizer = optim.Adam(human_model.parameters(), lr=0.001)
    ai_optimizer = optim.Adam(ai_model.parameters(), lr=0.001)

    human_criterion = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()
    ai_criterion = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()

    for epoch in range(n_epochs):
        # Training phase - train both models
        human_model.train()
        ai_model.train()

        # Train human model
        human_optimizer.zero_grad()
        train_logits = human_model(X_train_t, complexity_train_t).squeeze()
        train_loss_human = human_criterion(train_logits, y_train_t)
        train_loss_human.backward()
        human_optimizer.step()

        # Train AI model
        ai_optimizer.zero_grad()
        ai_train_logits = ai_model(X_train_t).squeeze()
        train_loss_ai = ai_criterion(ai_train_logits, y_train_t)
        train_loss_ai.backward()
        ai_optimizer.step()

        # Evaluation phase
        if (epoch + 1) % 10 == 0:
            # Simulate interaction on test set
            test_results = simulate_interaction(
                human_model,
                ai_model,
                X_test_t,
                y_test_t,
                complexity_test_t,
                mode,
                adaptive_threshold=0.6,
            )

            # Compute validation losses
            human_model.eval()
            ai_model.eval()
            with torch.no_grad():
                val_logits_human = human_model(X_test_t, complexity_test_t).squeeze()
                val_loss_human = human_criterion(val_logits_human, y_test_t)

                val_logits_ai = ai_model(X_test_t).squeeze()
                val_loss_ai = ai_criterion(val_logits_ai, y_test_t)

            # Store metrics
            experiment_data[mode]["epochs"].append(epoch + 1)
            experiment_data[mode]["losses"]["human"].append(val_loss_human.item())
            experiment_data[mode]["losses"]["ai"].append(val_loss_ai.item())
            experiment_data[mode]["metrics"]["appropriate_reliance"].append(
                test_results["appropriate_reliance"]
            )
            experiment_data[mode]["metrics"]["decision_accuracy"].append(
                test_results["decision_accuracy"]
            )
            experiment_data[mode]["metrics"]["agreement_rate"].append(
                test_results["agreement_rate"]
            )
            experiment_data[mode]["metrics"]["ai_accuracy"].append(
                test_results["ai_accuracy"]
            )
            experiment_data[mode]["cognitive_loads"].append(
                complexity_test_t.mean().item()
            )

            print(
                f"Epoch {epoch+1}: human_loss={val_loss_human.item():.4f}, ai_loss={val_loss_ai.item():.4f}, "
                f"ai_acc={test_results['ai_accuracy']:.4f}, "
                f"appropriate_reliance={test_results['appropriate_reliance']:.4f}, "
                f"decision_accuracy={test_results['decision_accuracy']:.4f}, "
                f"agreement_rate={test_results['agreement_rate']:.4f}"
            )

# Final evaluation and comparison
print("\n" + "=" * 80)
print("FINAL RESULTS COMPARISON (JOINT TRAINING)")
print("=" * 80)

final_results = {}
for mode in modes:
    final_results[mode] = {
        "appropriate_reliance": experiment_data[mode]["metrics"][
            "appropriate_reliance"
        ][-1],
        "decision_accuracy": experiment_data[mode]["metrics"]["decision_accuracy"][-1],
        "agreement_rate": experiment_data[mode]["metrics"]["agreement_rate"][-1],
        "ai_accuracy": experiment_data[mode]["metrics"]["ai_accuracy"][-1],
        "final_human_loss": experiment_data[mode]["losses"]["human"][-1],
        "final_ai_loss": experiment_data[mode]["losses"]["ai"][-1],
    }
    print(f"\n{mode.upper()} AI:")
    print(
        f"  Appropriate Reliance Score: {final_results[mode]['appropriate_reliance']:.4f}"
    )
    print(f"  Decision Accuracy: {final_results[mode]['decision_accuracy']:.4f}")
    print(f"  Agreement Rate: {final_results[mode]['agreement_rate']:.4f}")
    print(f"  AI Accuracy: {final_results[mode]['ai_accuracy']:.4f}")
    print(f"  Final Human Loss: {final_results[mode]['final_human_loss']:.4f}")
    print(f"  Final AI Loss: {final_results[mode]['final_ai_loss']:.4f}")

# Visualizations
print("\nGenerating visualizations...")

# Plot 1: Appropriate Reliance Score over epochs
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
for mode in modes:
    ax.plot(
        experiment_data[mode]["epochs"],
        experiment_data[mode]["metrics"]["appropriate_reliance"],
        marker="o",
        label=mode.capitalize(),
        linewidth=2,
    )
ax.set_xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("Appropriate Reliance Score", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title(
    "Appropriate Reliance Score (Joint Training - No AI Pre-training)",
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight="bold",
)
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(working_dir, "appropriate_reliance_comparison.png"), dpi=300)
plt.close()

# Plot 2: Decision Accuracy over epochs
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
for mode in modes:
    ax.plot(
        experiment_data[mode]["epochs"],
        experiment_data[mode]["metrics"]["decision_accuracy"],
        marker="s",
        label=mode.capitalize(),
        linewidth=2,
    )
ax.set_xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("Decision Accuracy", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title(
    "Decision Accuracy (Joint Training - No AI Pre-training)",
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight="bold",
)
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(working_dir, "decision_accuracy_comparison.png"), dpi=300)
plt.close()

# Plot 3: Agreement Rate over epochs
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
for mode in modes:
    ax.plot(
        experiment_data[mode]["epochs"],
        experiment_data[mode]["metrics"]["agreement_rate"],
        marker="^",
        label=mode.capitalize(),
        linewidth=2,
    )
ax.set_xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("Agreement Rate with AI", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title(
    "Human-AI Agreement Rate (Joint Training - No AI Pre-training)",
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight="bold",
)
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(working_dir, "agreement_rate_comparison.png"), dpi=300)
plt.close()

# Plot 4: AI Accuracy Evolution over epochs (NEW - shows co-evolution)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
for mode in modes:
    ax.plot(
        experiment_data[mode]["epochs"],
        experiment_data[mode]["metrics"]["ai_accuracy"],
        marker="d",
        label=mode.capitalize(),
        linewidth=2,
    )
ax.set_xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("AI Model Accuracy", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title(
    "AI Model Accuracy Evolution (Joint Training)", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold"
)
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(working_dir, "ai_accuracy_evolution.png"), dpi=300)
plt.close()

# Plot 5: Bar chart of final metrics
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(18, 5))
metrics_to_plot = [
    "appropriate_reliance",

]
metric_labels = [
    "Appropriate Reliance",

for idx, (metric, label) in enumerate(zip(metrics_to_plot, metric_labels)):
    values = [final_results[mode][metric] for mode in modes]
    bars = axes[idx].bar(
        modes, values, color=["#2ecc71", "#e74c3c", "#3498db"], alpha=0.8
    )

# Human loss
for mode in modes:

# AI loss
for mode in modes:
    axes[1].plot(
        experiment_data[mode]["epochs"],
        experiment_data[mode]["losses"]["ai"],
        marker="s",

)

axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Save all experiment data
np.save(os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), experiment_data)
print(
    f"\nAll experiment data saved to {os.path.join(working_dir, 'experiment_data.npy')}"

print("Generated plots:")

print("  - decision_accuracy_comparison.png")

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")

try:
    experiment_data = np.load(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), allow_pickle=True

if experiment_data:
    modes = ["supportive", "challenging", "adaptive"]

    # Plot 1: Appropriate Reliance Score Evolution

    # Plot 3: AI Accuracy Evolution

        plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
        for mode in modes:
            plt.plot(
                experiment_data[mode]["epochs"],
                experiment_data[mode]["metrics"]["ai_accuracy"],

                linewidth=2,
                color=colors[mode],

        plt.xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=12)
        plt.ylabel("AI Model Accuracy", fontsize=12)
        plt.title(
            "AI Model Accuracy Evolution - Joint Training\nDecision-Making Task Dataset",
            fontsize=13,
            fontweight="bold",
        )
        plt.legend(fontsize=11)
        plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.savefig(
            os.path.join(working_dir, "decision_task_ai_accuracy_evolution.png"),
            dpi=300,
        )
        plt.close()
        print("Created AI accuracy evolution plot")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error creating AI accuracy plot: {e}")
        plt.close()

    # Plot 4: Agreement Rate Evolution
    try:
        plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
        for mode in modes:
            plt.plot(
                experiment_data[mode]["epochs"],
                experiment_data[mode]["metrics"]["agreement_rate"],
                marker="^",
                label=mode.capitalize(),
                linewidth=2,
                color=colors[mode],
            )
        plt.xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=12)
        plt.ylabel("Agreement Rate", fontsize=12)
        plt.title(
            "Human-AI Agreement Rate Evolution - Joint Training\nDecision-Making Task Dataset",
            fontsize=13,
            fontweight="bold",
        )
        plt.legend(fontsize=11)
        plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.savefig(
            os.path.join(working_dir, "decision_task_agreement_rate_evolution.png"),
            dpi=300,
        )
        plt.close()
        print("Created agreement rate evolution plot")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error creating agreement rate plot: {e}")
        plt.close()

    # Plot 5: Loss Evolution (Human and AI side by side)
    try:
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6))
        for mode in modes:
            axes[0].plot(
                experiment_data[mode]["epochs"],
                experiment_data[mode]["losses"]["human"],
                marker="o",
                label=mode.capitalize(),
                linewidth=2,
                color=colors[mode],
            )
            axes[1].plot(
                experiment_data[mode]["epochs"],
                experiment_data[mode]["losses"]["ai"],
                marker="s",
                label=mode.capitalize(),
                linewidth=2,
                color=colors[mode],
            )

        axes[0].set_xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=12)
        axes[0].set_ylabel("Loss", fontsize=12)
        axes[0].set_title("Human Model Loss", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
        axes[0].legend(fontsize=11)
        axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

        axes[1].set_xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=12)
        axes[1].set_ylabel("Loss", fontsize=12)
        axes[1].set_title("AI Model Loss", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
        axes[1].legend(fontsize=11)
        axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

        fig.suptitle(
            "Model Loss Evolution - Joint Training\nDecision-Making Task Dataset",
            fontsize=14,
            fontweight="bold",
            y=1.02,
        )
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.savefig(
            os.path.join(working_dir, "decision_task_loss_evolution.png"), dpi=300
        )
        plt.close()
        print("Created loss evolution plot")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error creating loss evolution plot: {e}")
        plt.close()
plot_plan: I will create visualizations comparing the three AI interaction modes (supportive, challenging, and adaptive) across key metrics in this human-AI collaborative decision-making experiment. The plots will show how appropriate reliance, decision accuracy, agreement rates, and AI accuracy evolve during joint training where both human and AI models learn together from scratch. I'll visualize training curves for all metrics, final performance comparisons, and the co-evolution of both model losses to demonstrate how the interaction mode affects the development of effective human-AI collaboration.

plot_analyses.analysis: Bar-summary across modes shows a strong decoupling between raw accuracy and reliance calibration. Appropriate Reliance: adaptive (0.674) > supportive (0.605) > challenging (0.499). Yet Decision Accuracy is uniformly high and very close: challenging (0.952) ≈ adaptive (0.948) ≈ supportive (0.945). Agreement Rate is extremely high for all, but highest for challenging (0.998) vs supportive (0.973) and adaptive (0.967), indicating challenging induces near-always compliance. AI Accuracy is also high but slightly lower for adaptive (0.935) than supportive (0.948) and challenging (0.953). Interpretation: challenging’s near-perfect agreement despite only modest AI accuracy implies systematic overreliance (agreeing even when AI is wrong), which collapses Appropriate Reliance. Adaptive achieves the best reliance calibration despite slightly lower AI accuracy, suggesting the switching policy (or interaction dynamics) improves users’ selective acceptance/rejection. Supportive sits in between—high agreement but not as extreme, yielding moderate reliance calibration.

Decision Accuracy over epochs (joint training, no AI pre-training) shows all modes converge to ~0.95 by ~80–100 epochs. Early training differs: supportive starts higher (~0.79 at epoch 10) than adaptive (~0.76) and especially challenging (~0.70), but challenging/adaptive catch up and slightly edge supportive at convergence (challenging ~0.952, adaptive ~0.949–0.950, supportive ~0.945). This suggests the challenging and adaptive interaction policies may provide a stronger learning signal over time (or reduce early shortcut reliance), but the effect is small at convergence. Practical takeaway: if compute/epochs are limited, supportive gives better early performance; with sufficient training, mode differences in decision accuracy largely vanish, so reliance metrics become the differentiator.

Appropriate Reliance over epochs (joint training, no AI pre-training) reveals stark mode separation and non-monotonic dynamics. Challenging is essentially flat at ~0.50 throughout (minor bump to ~0.515–0.518 around 70–90), indicating persistent miscalibration/overreliance that training does not fix. Supportive rises to a peak ~0.81 around epoch 30 then steadily degrades to ~0.60 by epochs 70–100, suggesting later training increases agreement/automation bias or reduces users’ discrimination of AI correctness. Adaptive is more stable: fluctuates ~0.68–0.75 with a mild downward drift after ~60, ending ~0.67—consistently above supportive late in training and far above challenging. Key insight: optimizing for decision accuracy alone can harm appropriate reliance (supportive’s late-stage collapse), while adaptation acts as a regularizer maintaining calibration. This is important for ablations: components that improve accuracy may still worsen reliance; you need multi-objective selection/early stopping on reliance.

Human-AI agreement over epochs (joint training, no AI pre-training) shows challenging at ~1.0 across all epochs (near-total agreement), supportive and adaptive ramp from ~0.72/0.67 at epoch 10 to ~0.96–0.97 by epoch 70–100. This explains the reliance results: challenging’s agreement is saturated, leaving no room for selective disagreement when AI is wrong, hence low appropriate reliance. Supportive/adaptive increasing agreement over time aligns with the observed decline in supportive appropriate reliance after epoch ~40: as agreement approaches saturation, calibration can worsen unless users retain a mechanism to reject incorrect AI. Adaptive reaches similar agreement to supportive late, but maintains higher appropriate reliance, implying its disagreements are better targeted (higher precision of disagreement) rather than simply more frequent.

Loss curves (human model loss and AI model loss) decrease smoothly and similarly across modes, ending around ~0.12–0.13 by epoch 100, with only small separations (supportive slightly lower AI loss late; human loss nearly identical). This indicates optimization stability and that the large reliance differences are not due to gross training failure. It also suggests that architectural ablations should be evaluated primarily on reliance/behavioral metrics, because standard loss/accuracy may be insensitive. If you remove modules (attention/fusion/confidence), you may see minimal loss changes but substantial reliance shifts—so include reliance-driven validation/early stopping.

Agreement-rate evolution (joint training, decision-making dataset) mirrors the earlier agreement plot: challenging remains ~1.0; supportive and adaptive rise to ~0.96–0.97. Minor crossing behavior around epochs 60–80 suggests supportive slightly exceeds adaptive at some points, but differences are small. The key pattern is the ceiling effect for challenging and near-ceiling for the others, implying agreement rate alone is not a good target metric; it needs to be decomposed into agreement-when-AI-correct vs disagreement-when-AI-wrong (the core of appropriate reliance).

Appropriate reliance evolution (joint training, decision-making dataset) matches the earlier reliance plot with clearer emphasis: supportive peaks early (~0.81 at epoch 30) then drops sharply after epoch 40 to ~0.60; adaptive stays in a narrower band (~0.67–0.75) and overtakes supportive after ~50; challenging stays ~0.50. This suggests an interaction between training stage and reliance calibration: later epochs may overfit to AI suggestions or reduce human exploration. For paper claims, report both best-achieved reliance (supportive best at mid-training) and final/late-stage reliance (adaptive best), and justify a selection criterion (e.g., choose checkpoint maximizing reliance on validation).

Human decision accuracy evolution (joint training, decision-making dataset) shows convergence to ~0.95 for all modes, with supportive higher early, challenging lowest early but catching up and slightly surpassing at the end (~0.952–0.953). Adaptive tracks between early and ends ~0.95. Again, accuracy differences are small relative to reliance differences; challenging can yield high accuracy while still being behaviorally undesirable (overreliance). This supports the bidirectional-alignment framing: accuracy is insufficient; reliance calibration is the key outcome.

AI model accuracy evolution (joint training, decision-making dataset) increases for all modes; supportive and challenging end around ~0.95–0.955, adaptive ends lower (~0.935–0.94) with slight late dip/plateau. Despite lower AI accuracy, adaptive yields better appropriate reliance than supportive late, reinforcing that adaptive’s benefit is not from a stronger AI but from interaction policy shaping human use. This is a strong argument for your hypothesis: dynamic assertiveness can improve collaboration metrics even when the underlying AI is not the best-performing classifier.

Loss evolution (human and AI) duplicated in a cleaner format confirms: all modes have near-identical human loss trajectories; AI loss for supportive is marginally lower late, challenging slightly higher early, adaptive close. Given adaptive’s lower AI accuracy in the accuracy plot, check whether the loss is computed on a different split/target or whether calibration/thresholding differs; otherwise, the discrepancy may indicate class imbalance effects or that accuracy is measured differently than loss. In ablations, track calibration metrics (ECE/Brier) for AI and human confidence, because loss alone won’t capture the reliance behavior.

vlm_feedback_summary: Plots consistently show that interaction mode primarily affects reliance calibration rather than raw decision accuracy. Challenging drives near-100% agreement, yielding high accuracy but very low appropriate reliance (~0.50), indicative of overreliance. Supportive achieves moderate reliance but degrades over training as agreement saturates. Adaptive maintains the best and most stable appropriate reliance (~0.67–0.75 over epochs) despite slightly lower AI accuracy, suggesting the switching policy improves selective acceptance/rejection. Loss curves are similar across modes, implying behavioral metrics are essential for ablation conclusions.

overall_plan:
Ablation name: Remove Dropout Regularization from Human Decision Model.
I will implement an ablation study that removes the dropout layer from the Human Decision Model. The approach involves creating a modified HumanDecisionModel class without dropout, then running the same three interaction modes (supportive, challenging, adaptive) with this ablation version. I'll compare the results between the baseline (with dropout) and the ablation (without dropout) to assess whether dropout regularization affects human-AI collaboration patterns. The experiment data will be structured to store both baseline and ablation results for direct comparison, and visualizations will show the impact of removing dropout on appropriate reliance, decision accuracy, and agreement rates across all interaction modes.

Final appropriate reliance score measuring how well the model relies on AI assistance appropriately
Final decision accuracy for each model and interaction mode
Final agreement rate between model decisions and AI assistance

code:
import os
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Setup
working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")
os.makedirs(working_dir, exist_ok=True)

device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
print(f"Using device: {device}")

# Set random seeds
np.random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# Initialize experiment data structure
experiment_data = {
    "baseline": {
        "supportive": {
            "metrics": {
                "appropriate_reliance": [],
                "decision_accuracy": [],
                "agreement_rate": [],
            },
            "losses": [],
            "epochs": [],
        },
        "challenging": {
            "metrics": {
                "appropriate_reliance": [],
                "decision_accuracy": [],
                "agreement_rate": [],
            },
            "losses": [],
            "epochs": [],
        },
        "adaptive": {
            "metrics": {
                "appropriate_reliance": [],
                "decision_accuracy": [],
                "agreement_rate": [],
            },
            "losses": [],
            "epochs": [],
        },
    },
    "ablation_no_dropout": {
        "supportive": {
            "metrics": {
                "appropriate_reliance": [],
                "decision_accuracy": [],
                "agreement_rate": [],
            },
            "losses": [],
            "epochs": [],
        },
        "challenging": {
            "metrics": {
                "appropriate_reliance": [],
                "decision_accuracy": [],
                "agreement_rate": [],
            },
            "losses": [],
            "epochs": [],
        },
        "adaptive": {
            "metrics": {
                "appropriate_reliance": [],
                "decision_accuracy": [],
                "agreement_rate": [],
            },
            "losses": [],
            "epochs": [],
        },
    },
}


# Generate synthetic dataset
def generate_decision_task_data(n_samples=2000, n_features=20):
    X = np.random.randn(n_samples, n_features).astype(np.float32)
    weights = np.random.randn(n_features)
    logits = X @ weights + np.random.randn(n_samples) * 0.5
    y_true = (logits > 0).astype(np.float32)
    task_complexity = np.abs(logits) / (np.abs(logits).max())
    task_complexity = 1 - task_complexity
    return X, y_true, task_complexity


# Baseline: Human decision model with dropout
class HumanDecisionModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim=20, hidden_dim=64):
        super(HumanDecisionModel, self).__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc3 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(0.3)

    def forward(self, x, cognitive_load=None):
        x = torch.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = self.dropout(x)
        x = torch.relu(self.fc2(x))
        if cognitive_load is not None and self.training:
            noise = torch.randn_like(x) * cognitive_load.unsqueeze(1) * 0.5
            x = x + noise
        x = self.fc3(x)
        return x


# Ablation: Human decision model WITHOUT dropout
class HumanDecisionModelNoDropout(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim=20, hidden_dim=64):
        super(HumanDecisionModelNoDropout, self).__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc3 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1)

    def forward(self, x, cognitive_load=None):
        x = torch.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = torch.relu(self.fc2(x))
        if cognitive_load is not None and self.training:
            noise = torch.randn_like(x) * cognitive_load.unsqueeze(1) * 0.5
            x = x + noise
        x = self.fc3(x)
        return x


# AI assistant model
class AIAssistantModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim=20, hidden_dim=64):
        super(AIAssistantModel, self).__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc3 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = torch.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = torch.relu(self.fc2(x))
        x = self.fc3(x)
        return x


# Generate data
print("Generating synthetic decision-making data...")
X, y_true, task_complexity = generate_decision_task_data(n_samples=2000, n_features=20)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test, complexity_train, complexity_test = train_test_split(
    X, y_true, task_complexity, test_size=0.3, random_state=42
)

X_train_t = torch.FloatTensor(X_train).to(device)
y_train_t = torch.FloatTensor(y_train).to(device)
complexity_train_t = torch.FloatTensor(complexity_train).to(device)
X_test_t = torch.FloatTensor(X_test).to(device)
y_test_t = torch.FloatTensor(y_test).to(device)
complexity_test_t = torch.FloatTensor(complexity_test).to(device)

# Train AI assistant model
print("\nTraining AI Assistant model...")
ai_model = AIAssistantModel(input_dim=20, hidden_dim=64).to(device)
ai_optimizer = optim.Adam(ai_model.parameters(), lr=0.001)
ai_criterion = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()

for epoch in range(50):
    ai_model.train()
    ai_optimizer.zero_grad()
    ai_logits = ai_model(X_train_t).squeeze()
    ai_loss = ai_criterion(ai_logits, y_train_t)
    ai_loss.backward()
    ai_optimizer.step()

    if (epoch + 1) % 10 == 0:
        ai_model.eval()
        with torch.no_grad():
            test_logits = ai_model(X_test_t).squeeze()
            test_loss = ai_criterion(test_logits, y_test_t)
            test_acc = ((torch.sigmoid(test_logits) > 0.5) == y_test_t).float().mean()
            print(
                f"AI Assistant Epoch {epoch+1}: train_loss={ai_loss.item():.4f}, test_acc={test_acc.item():.4f}"
            )


# Simulate human-AI interaction
def simulate_interaction(
    human_model,
    ai_model,
    X,
    y_true,
    cognitive_load,
    interaction_mode,
    adaptive_threshold=0.6,
):
    human_model.eval()
    ai_model.eval()

    with torch.no_grad():
        human_logits = human_model(X, cognitive_load).squeeze()
        human_initial = torch.sigmoid(human_logits) > 0.5
        human_confidence = torch.abs(torch.sigmoid(human_logits) - 0.5) * 2

        ai_logits = ai_model(X).squeeze()
        ai_recommendation = torch.sigmoid(ai_logits) > 0.5
        ai_correct = ai_recommendation == y_true

        if interaction_mode == "supportive":
            ai_challenges = torch.zeros_like(human_initial, dtype=torch.bool)
        elif interaction_mode == "challenging":
            ai_challenges = ai_recommendation != human_initial
        else:  # adaptive
            high_cognitive_load = cognitive_load > adaptive_threshold
            ai_challenges = (ai_recommendation != human_initial) & (
                ~high_cognitive_load
            )

        change_decision = ai_challenges & (human_confidence < 0.6)
        human_final = human_initial.clone()
        human_final[change_decision] = ai_recommendation[change_decision]

        agreement_with_ai = (human_final == ai_recommendation).float()

        agreement_when_ai_correct = (
            agreement_with_ai[ai_correct].mean()
            if ai_correct.sum() > 0
            else torch.tensor(0.0)
        )
        disagreement_when_ai_incorrect = (
            (1 - agreement_with_ai[~ai_correct]).mean()
            if (~ai_correct).sum() > 0
            else torch.tensor(0.0)
        )
        appropriate_reliance = (
            agreement_when_ai_correct + disagreement_when_ai_incorrect
        ) / 2

        decision_accuracy = (human_final == y_true).float().mean()
        agreement_rate = agreement_with_ai.mean()

    return {
        "appropriate_reliance": appropriate_reliance.item(),
        "decision_accuracy": decision_accuracy.item(),
        "agreement_rate": agreement_rate.item(),
    }


# Train and evaluate both baseline and ablation models
print("\n" + "=" * 80)
print("ABLATION STUDY: Comparing WITH and WITHOUT Dropout")
print("=" * 80)

modes = ["supportive", "challenging", "adaptive"]
n_epochs = 100
model_types = [
    ("baseline", HumanDecisionModel),
    ("ablation_no_dropout", HumanDecisionModelNoDropout),
]

for model_type, ModelClass in model_types:
    print(f"\n{'='*80}")
    print(f"Training {model_type.upper()} model")
    print(f"{'='*80}")

    for mode in modes:
        print(f"\n{mode.upper()} AI interaction mode:")

        # Reset seed for fair comparison
        torch.manual_seed(42)
        np.random.seed(42)

        human_model = ModelClass(input_dim=20, hidden_dim=64).to(device)
        human_optimizer = optim.Adam(human_model.parameters(), lr=0.001)
        human_criterion = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()

        for epoch in range(n_epochs):
            human_model.train()
            human_optimizer.zero_grad()
            train_logits = human_model(X_train_t, complexity_train_t).squeeze()
            train_loss = human_criterion(train_logits, y_train_t)
            train_loss.backward()
            human_optimizer.step()

            if (epoch + 1) % 10 == 0:
                test_results = simulate_interaction(
                    human_model,
                    ai_model,
                    X_test_t,
                    y_test_t,
                    complexity_test_t,
                    mode,
                    adaptive_threshold=0.6,
                )

                human_model.eval()
                with torch.no_grad():
                    val_logits = human_model(X_test_t, complexity_test_t).squeeze()
                    val_loss = human_criterion(val_logits, y_test_t)

                experiment_data[model_type][mode]["epochs"].append(epoch + 1)
                experiment_data[model_type][mode]["losses"].append(val_loss.item())
                experiment_data[model_type][mode]["metrics"][
                    "appropriate_reliance"
                ].append(test_results["appropriate_reliance"])
                experiment_data[model_type][mode]["metrics"][
                    "decision_accuracy"
                ].append(test_results["decision_accuracy"])
                experiment_data[model_type][mode]["metrics"]["agreement_rate"].append(
                    test_results["agreement_rate"]
                )

                print(
                    f"Epoch {epoch+1}: val_loss={val_loss.item():.4f}, approp_reliance={test_results['appropriate_reliance']:.4f}, accuracy={test_results['decision_accuracy']:.4f}"
                )

# Final results comparison
print("\n" + "=" * 80)
print("FINAL RESULTS COMPARISON")
print("=" * 80)

for model_type in ["baseline", "ablation_no_dropout"]:
    print(f"\n{model_type.upper()}:")
    for mode in modes:
        final_reliance = experiment_data[model_type][mode]["metrics"][
            "appropriate_reliance"
        ][-1]
        final_accuracy = experiment_data[model_type][mode]["metrics"][
            "decision_accuracy"
        ][-1]
        final_agreement = experiment_data[model_type][mode]["metrics"][
            "agreement_rate"
        ][-1]
        print(
            f"  {mode.capitalize()}: Reliance={final_reliance:.4f}, Accuracy={final_accuracy:.4f}, Agreement={final_agreement:.4f}"
        )

# Visualizations
print("\nGenerating visualizations...")

# Plot 1: Appropriate Reliance Comparison
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6))
for idx, model_type in enumerate(["baseline", "ablation_no_dropout"]):
    for mode in modes:
        axes[idx].plot(
            experiment_data[model_type][mode]["epochs"],
            experiment_data[model_type][mode]["metrics"]["appropriate_reliance"],
            marker="o",
            label=mode.capitalize(),
            linewidth=2,
        )
    axes[idx].set_xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=12)
    axes[idx].set_ylabel("Appropriate Reliance Score", fontsize=12)
    title = (
        "Baseline (With Dropout)"
        if model_type == "baseline"
        else "Ablation (No Dropout)"
    )
    axes[idx].set_title(title, fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
    axes[idx].legend(fontsize=10)
    axes[idx].grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(working_dir, "ablation_appropriate_reliance.png"), dpi=300)
plt.close()

# Plot 2: Decision Accuracy Comparison
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6))
for idx, model_type in enumerate(["baseline", "ablation_no_dropout"]):
    for mode in modes:
        axes[idx].plot(
            experiment_data[model_type][mode]["epochs"],
            experiment_data[model_type][mode]["metrics"]["decision_accuracy"],
            marker="s",
            label=mode.capitalize(),
            linewidth=2,
        )
    axes[idx].set_xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=12)
    axes[idx].set_ylabel("Decision Accuracy", fontsize=12)
    title = (
        "Baseline (With Dropout)"
        if model_type == "baseline"
        else "Ablation (No Dropout)"
    )
    axes[idx].set_title(title, fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
    axes[idx].legend(fontsize=10)
    axes[idx].grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(working_dir, "ablation_decision_accuracy.png"), dpi=300)
plt.close()

# Plot 3: Bar chart comparison - Final metrics across all conditions
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))
metrics_to_plot = ["appropriate_reliance", "decision_accuracy", "agreement_rate"]
metric_labels = ["Appropriate Reliance", "Decision Accuracy", "Agreement Rate"]

x_positions = np.arange(len(modes))
width = 0.35

for idx, (metric, label) in enumerate(zip(metrics_to_plot, metric_labels)):
    baseline_values = [
        experiment_data["baseline"][mode]["metrics"][metric][-1] for mode in modes
    ]
    ablation_values = [
        experiment_data["ablation_no_dropout"][mode]["metrics"][metric][-1]
        for mode in modes
    ]

    axes[idx].bar(
        x_positions - width / 2,
        baseline_values,
        width,
        label="Baseline (With Dropout)",
        alpha=0.8,
        color="#3498db",
    )
    axes[idx].bar(
        x_positions + width / 2,
        ablation_values,
        width,
        label="Ablation (No Dropout)",
        alpha=0.8,
        color="#e74c3c",
    )

    axes[idx].set_ylabel(label, fontsize=11)
    axes[idx].set_ylim([0, 1])
    axes[idx].set_title(label, fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
    axes[idx].set_xticks(x_positions)
    axes[idx].set_xticklabels([m.capitalize() for m in modes])
    axes[idx].legend(fontsize=9)
    axes[idx].grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis="y")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(working_dir, "ablation_final_comparison.png"), dpi=300)
plt.close()

# Plot 4: Difference plot showing impact of dropout removal
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))
for idx, (metric, label) in enumerate(zip(metrics_to_plot, metric_labels)):
    differences = []
    for mode in modes:
        baseline_val = experiment_data["baseline"][mode]["metrics"][metric][-1]
        ablation_val = experiment_data["ablation_no_dropout"][mode]["metrics"][metric][
            -1
        ]
        differences.append(ablation_val - baseline_val)

    colors = ["#2ecc71" if d > 0 else "#e74c3c" for d in differences]
    bars = axes[idx].bar(modes, differences, color=colors, alpha=0.8)
    axes[idx].axhline(y=0, color="black", linestyle="-", linewidth=0.8)
    axes[idx].set_ylabel(f"Δ {label}", fontsize=11)
    axes[idx].set_title(
        f"Impact of Removing Dropout\n({label})", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold"
    )
    axes[idx].grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis="y")

    for bar, diff in zip(bars, differences):
        height = bar.get_height()
        axes[idx].text(
            bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2.0,
            height,
            f"{diff:+.3f}",
            ha="center",
            va="bottom" if height > 0 else "top",
            fontsize=10,
        )

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(working_dir, "ablation_impact_differences.png"), dpi=300)
plt.close()

# Save experiment data
np.save(os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), experiment_data)
print(
    f"\nAll experiment data saved to {os.path.join(working_dir, 'experiment_data.npy')}"
)

print("\n" + "=" * 80)
print("ABLATION STUDY COMPLETE")
print("=" * 80)
print(f"Visualizations saved to: {working_dir}")
print("Generated plots:")
print("  - ablation_appropriate_reliance.png")
print("  - ablation_decision_accuracy.png")
print("  - ablation_final_comparison.png")
print("  - ablation_impact_differences.png")

plot_plan: I'll create visualizations comparing the baseline model (with dropout) and the ablation model (without dropout) across different AI interaction modes. The experiment evaluates human-AI decision-making with supportive, challenging, and adaptive AI strategies. I'll plot the training curves for appropriate reliance, decision accuracy, and agreement rates, as well as create comparison bar charts and difference plots to show the impact of removing dropout.

plot_analyses.analysis: Dropout removal impact summary (Δ metrics, decision-making dataset): Removing dropout slightly decreases Appropriate Reliance in Supportive (−0.009) and Adaptive (−0.009) modes, but increases it in Challenging (+0.018). Decision Accuracy changes are tiny: Supportive −0.002, Adaptive −0.002, Challenging +0.003. Agreement Rate changes are also tiny: Supportive +0.002, Adaptive +0.002, Challenging −0.003. Interpretation: dropout is not a uniformly beneficial regularizer here; it appears to help the reliance-calibration objective in Supportive/Adaptive, but hurts it in Challenging. The direction flip suggests an interaction between the challenging policy and representation noise/regularization—possibly dropout disrupts the model’s ability to produce consistent challenge cues that users can appropriately accept/reject, while in supportive/adaptive it may prevent overconfident/overfitted advice that induces mis-reliance.

Decision accuracy learning curves (with vs without dropout) by mode: Supportive and Adaptive show near-identical trajectories with and without dropout across epochs, converging around ~0.94–0.95; differences are visually negligible and consistent with the small final Δ (≈−0.002). Challenging shows a long plateau around ~0.908 through ~70 epochs for both variants, then late improvement; the no-dropout variant ends slightly higher than with-dropout, matching the +0.003 final gain. Interpretation: dropout does not materially affect convergence for supportive/adaptive, but in challenging it may slightly slow or cap late-stage fitting; alternatively, no-dropout may allow the model to exploit mode-specific signals that only become useful late in training.

Per-mode accuracy comparison plots (baseline with dropout vs ablation no dropout): The supportive curve is not shown here (only challenging and adaptive appear), but overall the baseline vs ablation panels indicate almost indistinguishable final accuracies, with ablation marginally higher for challenging and marginally lower/near-equal for adaptive. Interpretation: accuracy is robust to dropout removal; any reliance effects are unlikely driven by large accuracy shifts and more likely by calibration/interaction dynamics.

Appropriate reliance learning curves (with vs without dropout) by mode: Supportive and Adaptive: both improve substantially after ~50–60 epochs; the no-dropout variant dips more around epoch ~50 and ends slightly lower than with-dropout (consistent with −0.009). Challenging: both sit at ~0.50 for most of training, then rise late; no-dropout rises more, ending higher (consistent with +0.018). Interpretation: dropout seems to stabilize/benefit reliance in supportive/adaptive during the mid-training transition, but in challenging it suppresses the late-stage emergence of appropriate reliance. The long flatline at 0.50 in challenging suggests either (a) the reliance metric is effectively at chance until the model crosses a competence/calibration threshold, or (b) the challenging interaction policy dominates behavior until the model becomes sufficiently accurate/confident; dropout may delay reaching that regime.

Final metric bar comparisons (with dropout vs no dropout): Appropriate Reliance: supportive and adaptive slightly higher with dropout; challenging higher without dropout. Decision Accuracy: nearly identical, with challenging slightly higher without dropout. Agreement Rate: supportive/adaptive slightly higher without dropout; challenging slightly lower without dropout. Interpretation: For supportive/adaptive, dropout increases appropriate reliance despite slightly lower agreement—suggesting improved selective agreement (agree when correct, disagree when incorrect). For challenging, no-dropout increases appropriate reliance while decreasing agreement—again consistent with better selectivity rather than more compliance. This is aligned with the paper’s emphasis on appropriate reliance over raw agreement.

Agreement rate learning curves (with vs without dropout) by mode: Supportive and Adaptive show similar shapes: agreement rises to near 1.0 around mid-epochs (~50) then gradually declines toward ~0.94 by epoch 100; no-dropout is slightly lower early but similar at the end, matching +0.002 final gain. Challenging stays at ~1.0 agreement for many epochs, then drops late; no-dropout drops more by the end (≈0.98 vs ≈0.983), matching −0.003. Interpretation: Challenging mode encourages near-total agreement early (possibly due to interaction framing or model behavior), which is counter to “challenge” intent; only late training does disagreement emerge. The stronger late disagreement without dropout coincides with higher appropriate reliance, suggesting the system is learning to induce/permit user pushback when AI is wrong (or at least reduce blanket agreement).

Appropriate reliance across modes over epochs (baseline vs ablation): Baseline: challenging remains at 0.50 until ~80 then rises to ~0.59; adaptive climbs from ~0.52 to ~0.77 with a dip near ~50; supportive is not plotted here but earlier plot indicates it reaches ~0.76–0.77. Ablation: challenging similarly flat until ~70 then rises higher to ~0.61; adaptive follows similar trajectory but ends slightly lower than baseline. Interpretation: Dropout removal mainly affects the tail behavior (late-epoch regime) rather than early learning, and the effect is mode-specific. This supports reporting mode × component interactions in the ablation section rather than a single averaged “dropout helps/hurts” conclusion.

vlm_feedback_summary: Plots show dropout ablation on a decision-making dataset across supportive/challenging/adaptive modes. Removing dropout has minimal effect on decision accuracy (≈±0.003) and agreement (≈±0.003), but a mode-dependent effect on appropriate reliance: small decreases in supportive/adaptive (−0.009 each) and a notable increase in challenging (+0.018). Learning curves indicate challenging mode’s reliance and accuracy stay flat for many epochs then improve late; no-dropout improves the late-stage gains and yields lower final agreement but higher appropriate reliance, suggesting better selectivity rather than more compliance. Overall, dropout is not a critical component for accuracy, but it interacts with interaction mode in shaping reliance calibration, so it should be analyzed with mode-specific statistics and possibly tuned per mode rather than globally.

overall_plan:
Ablation name: Remove Human Decision Model Noise Injection (Deterministic Human Performance).
I will implement an ablation study that compares the baseline model (with noise injection) against a modified version where the noise injection mechanism is removed from the HumanDecisionModel. The ablation will train both versions across all three interaction modes (supportive, challenging, adaptive) and compare their performance on appropriate reliance, decision accuracy, and agreement rate metrics. This will isolate whether the benefits of adaptive AI interaction stem from modeling human performance variability under cognitive load versus just using cognitive load information. The results will show if deterministic human performance changes the effectiveness of different interaction modes.

code:
import os
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Setup
working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")
os.makedirs(working_dir, exist_ok=True)

device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
print(f"Using device: {device}")

# Set random seeds
np.random.seed(42)
torch.manual_seed(42)

# Initialize experiment data structure for ablation study
experiment_data = {
    "baseline": {  # With noise injection
        "supportive": {
            "metrics": {
                "appropriate_reliance": [],
                "decision_accuracy": [],
                "agreement_rate": [],
            },
            "losses": [],
            "epochs": [],
        },
        "challenging": {
            "metrics": {
                "appropriate_reliance": [],
                "decision_accuracy": [],
                "agreement_rate": [],
            },
            "losses": [],
            "epochs": [],
        },
        "adaptive": {
            "metrics": {
                "appropriate_reliance": [],
                "decision_accuracy": [],
                "agreement_rate": [],
            },
            "losses": [],
            "epochs": [],
        },
    },
    "ablation": {  # Without noise injection (deterministic)
        "supportive": {
            "metrics": {
                "appropriate_reliance": [],
                "decision_accuracy": [],
                "agreement_rate": [],
            },
            "losses": [],
            "epochs": [],
        },
        "challenging": {
            "metrics": {
                "appropriate_reliance": [],
                "decision_accuracy": [],
                "agreement_rate": [],
            },
            "losses": [],
            "epochs": [],
        },
        "adaptive": {
            "metrics": {
                "appropriate_reliance": [],
                "decision_accuracy": [],
                "agreement_rate": [],
            },
            "losses": [],
            "epochs": [],
        },
    },
}


def generate_decision_task_data(n_samples=2000, n_features=20):
    X = np.random.randn(n_samples, n_features).astype(np.float32)
    weights = np.random.randn(n_features)
    logits = X @ weights + np.random.randn(n_samples) * 0.5
    y_true = (logits > 0).astype(np.float32)
    task_complexity = np.abs(logits) / (np.abs(logits).max())
    task_complexity = 1 - task_complexity
    return X, y_true, task_complexity


class HumanDecisionModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim=20, hidden_dim=64, use_noise=True):
        super(HumanDecisionModel, self).__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc3 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(0.3)
        self.use_noise = use_noise

    def forward(self, x, cognitive_load=None):
        x = torch.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = self.dropout(x)
        x = torch.relu(self.fc2(x))

        # Ablation: only add noise if use_noise is True
        if self.use_noise and cognitive_load is not None and self.training:
            noise = torch.randn_like(x) * cognitive_load.unsqueeze(1) * 0.5
            x = x + noise

        x = self.fc3(x)
        return x


class AIAssistantModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim=20, hidden_dim=64):
        super(AIAssistantModel, self).__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, hidden_dim)
        self.fc3 = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, 1)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = torch.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = torch.relu(self.fc2(x))
        x = self.fc3(x)
        return x


def simulate_interaction(
    human_model,
    ai_model,
    X,
    y_true,
    cognitive_load,
    interaction_mode,
    adaptive_threshold=0.6,
):
    human_model.eval()
    ai_model.eval()

    with torch.no_grad():
        human_logits = human_model(X, cognitive_load).squeeze()
        human_initial = torch.sigmoid(human_logits) > 0.5
        human_confidence = torch.abs(torch.sigmoid(human_logits) - 0.5) * 2

        ai_logits = ai_model(X).squeeze()
        ai_recommendation = torch.sigmoid(ai_logits) > 0.5
        ai_correct = ai_recommendation == y_true

        if interaction_mode == "supportive":
            ai_challenges = torch.zeros_like(human_initial, dtype=torch.bool)
        elif interaction_mode == "challenging":
            ai_challenges = ai_recommendation != human_initial
        else:  # adaptive
            high_cognitive_load = cognitive_load > adaptive_threshold
            ai_challenges = (ai_recommendation != human_initial) & (
                ~high_cognitive_load
            )

        change_decision = ai_challenges & (human_confidence < 0.6)
        human_final = human_initial.clone()
        human_final[change_decision] = ai_recommendation[change_decision]

        agreement_with_ai = (human_final == ai_recommendation).float()
        agreement_when_ai_correct = (
            agreement_with_ai[ai_correct].mean()
            if ai_correct.sum() > 0
            else torch.tensor(0.0)
        )
        disagreement_when_ai_incorrect = (
            (1 - agreement_with_ai[~ai_correct]).mean()
            if (~ai_correct).sum() > 0
            else torch.tensor(0.0)
        )
        appropriate_reliance = (
            agreement_when_ai_correct + disagreement_when_ai_incorrect
        ) / 2
        decision_accuracy = (human_final == y_true).float().mean()
        agreement_rate = agreement_with_ai.mean()

    return {
        "appropriate_reliance": appropriate_reliance.item(),
        "decision_accuracy": decision_accuracy.item(),
        "agreement_rate": agreement_rate.item(),
    }


# Generate data
print("Generating synthetic decision-making data...")
X, y_true, task_complexity = generate_decision_task_data(n_samples=2000, n_features=20)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test, complexity_train, complexity_test = train_test_split(
    X, y_true, task_complexity, test_size=0.3, random_state=42
)

X_train_t = torch.FloatTensor(X_train).to(device)
y_train_t = torch.FloatTensor(y_train).to(device)
complexity_train_t = torch.FloatTensor(complexity_train).to(device)
X_test_t = torch.FloatTensor(X_test).to(device)
y_test_t = torch.FloatTensor(y_test).to(device)
complexity_test_t = torch.FloatTensor(complexity_test).to(device)

# Train AI assistant model
print("\nTraining AI Assistant model...")
ai_model = AIAssistantModel(input_dim=20, hidden_dim=64).to(device)
ai_optimizer = optim.Adam(ai_model.parameters(), lr=0.001)
ai_criterion = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()

for epoch in range(50):
    ai_model.train()
    ai_optimizer.zero_grad()
    ai_logits = ai_model(X_train_t).squeeze()
    ai_loss = ai_criterion(ai_logits, y_train_t)
    ai_loss.backward()
    ai_optimizer.step()

print("AI Assistant training complete.")

# Ablation study: Train and evaluate both baseline and ablation versions
modes = ["supportive", "challenging", "adaptive"]
n_epochs = 100
conditions = [("baseline", True), ("ablation", False)]

for condition_name, use_noise in conditions:
    print(f"\n{'='*80}")
    print(f"CONDITION: {condition_name.upper()} (use_noise={use_noise})")
    print(f"{'='*80}")

    for mode in modes:
        print(f"\n{'-'*80}")
        print(f"Training with {mode.upper()} AI interaction mode")
        print(f"{'-'*80}")

        # Reset random seed for fair comparison
        torch.manual_seed(42)

        human_model = HumanDecisionModel(
            input_dim=20, hidden_dim=64, use_noise=use_noise
        ).to(device)
        human_optimizer = optim.Adam(human_model.parameters(), lr=0.001)
        human_criterion = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()

        for epoch in range(n_epochs):
            human_model.train()
            human_optimizer.zero_grad()
            train_logits = human_model(X_train_t, complexity_train_t).squeeze()
            train_loss = human_criterion(train_logits, y_train_t)
            train_loss.backward()
            human_optimizer.step()

            if (epoch + 1) % 10 == 0:
                test_results = simulate_interaction(
                    human_model,
                    ai_model,
                    X_test_t,
                    y_test_t,
                    complexity_test_t,
                    mode,
                    adaptive_threshold=0.6,
                )

                human_model.eval()
                with torch.no_grad():
                    val_logits = human_model(X_test_t, complexity_test_t).squeeze()
                    val_loss = human_criterion(val_logits, y_test_t)

                experiment_data[condition_name][mode]["epochs"].append(epoch + 1)
                experiment_data[condition_name][mode]["losses"].append(val_loss.item())
                experiment_data[condition_name][mode]["metrics"][
                    "appropriate_reliance"
                ].append(test_results["appropriate_reliance"])
                experiment_data[condition_name][mode]["metrics"][
                    "decision_accuracy"
                ].append(test_results["decision_accuracy"])
                experiment_data[condition_name][mode]["metrics"][
                    "agreement_rate"
                ].append(test_results["agreement_rate"])

                print(
                    f"Epoch {epoch+1}: val_loss={val_loss.item():.4f}, AR={test_results['appropriate_reliance']:.4f}, "
                    f"DA={test_results['decision_accuracy']:.4f}, AGR={test_results['agreement_rate']:.4f}"
                )

# Final results comparison
print("\n" + "=" * 80)
print("ABLATION STUDY: FINAL RESULTS COMPARISON")
print("=" * 80)

final_results = {}
for condition_name in ["baseline", "ablation"]:
    final_results[condition_name] = {}
    print(f"\n{condition_name.upper()} (use_noise={condition_name=='baseline'}):")
    for mode in modes:
        final_results[condition_name][mode] = {
            "appropriate_reliance": experiment_data[condition_name][mode]["metrics"][
                "appropriate_reliance"
            ][-1],
            "decision_accuracy": experiment_data[condition_name][mode]["metrics"][
                "decision_accuracy"
            ][-1],
            "agreement_rate": experiment_data[condition_name][mode]["metrics"][
                "agreement_rate"
            ][-1],
            "final_loss": experiment_data[condition_name][mode]["losses"][-1],
        }
        print(f"  {mode.upper()}:")
        print(
            f"    Appropriate Reliance: {final_results[condition_name][mode]['appropriate_reliance']:.4f}"
        )
        print(
            f"    Decision Accuracy: {final_results[condition_name][mode]['decision_accuracy']:.4f}"
        )
        print(
            f"    Agreement Rate: {final_results[condition_name][mode]['agreement_rate']:.4f}"
        )

# Visualizations
print("\nGenerating ablation study visualizations...")

# Plot 1: Appropriate Reliance comparison between baseline and ablation
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6))
for idx, condition_name in enumerate(["baseline", "ablation"]):
    for mode in modes:
        axes[idx].plot(
            experiment_data[condition_name][mode]["epochs"],
            experiment_data[condition_name][mode]["metrics"]["appropriate_reliance"],
            marker="o",
            label=mode.capitalize(),
            linewidth=2,
        )
    axes[idx].set_xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=12)
    axes[idx].set_ylabel("Appropriate Reliance Score", fontsize=12)
    title = (
        "Baseline (With Noise)"
        if condition_name == "baseline"
        else "Ablation (Without Noise)"
    )
    axes[idx].set_title(title, fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
    axes[idx].legend(fontsize=10)
    axes[idx].grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(working_dir, "ablation_appropriate_reliance.png"), dpi=300)
plt.close()

# Plot 2: Decision Accuracy comparison
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6))
for idx, condition_name in enumerate(["baseline", "ablation"]):
    for mode in modes:
        axes[idx].plot(
            experiment_data[condition_name][mode]["epochs"],
            experiment_data[condition_name][mode]["metrics"]["decision_accuracy"],
            marker="s",
            label=mode.capitalize(),
            linewidth=2,
        )
    axes[idx].set_xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=12)
    axes[idx].set_ylabel("Decision Accuracy", fontsize=12)
    title = (
        "Baseline (With Noise)"
        if condition_name == "baseline"
        else "Ablation (Without Noise)"
    )
    axes[idx].set_title(title, fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
    axes[idx].legend(fontsize=10)
    axes[idx].grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(working_dir, "ablation_decision_accuracy.png"), dpi=300)
plt.close()

# Plot 3: Agreement Rate comparison
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6))
for idx, condition_name in enumerate(["baseline", "ablation"]):
    for mode in modes:
        axes[idx].plot(
            experiment_data[condition_name][mode]["epochs"],
            experiment_data[condition_name][mode]["metrics"]["agreement_rate"],
            marker="^",
            label=mode.capitalize(),
            linewidth=2,
        )
    axes[idx].set_xlabel("Epoch", fontsize=12)
    axes[idx].set_ylabel("Agreement Rate", fontsize=12)
    title = (
        "Baseline (With Noise)"
        if condition_name == "baseline"
        else "Ablation (Without Noise)"
    )
    axes[idx].set_title(title, fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
    axes[idx].legend(fontsize=10)
    axes[idx].grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(working_dir, "ablation_agreement_rate.png"), dpi=300)
plt.close()

# Plot 4: Bar chart comparing final metrics across all conditions
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 6))
metrics_to_plot = ["appropriate_reliance", "decision_accuracy", "agreement_rate"]
metric_labels = ["Appropriate Reliance", "Decision Accuracy", "Agreement Rate"]
x_pos = np.arange(len(modes))
width = 0.35

for idx, (metric, label) in enumerate(zip(metrics_to_plot, metric_labels)):
    baseline_values = [final_results["baseline"][mode][metric] for mode in modes]
    ablation_values = [final_results["ablation"][mode][metric] for mode in modes]

    axes[idx].bar(
        x_pos - width / 2,
        baseline_values,
        width,
        label="Baseline (With Noise)",
        alpha=0.8,
        color="#3498db",
    )
    axes[idx].bar(
        x_pos + width / 2,
        ablation_values,
        width,
        label="Ablation (Without Noise)",
        alpha=0.8,
        color="#e74c3c",
    )

    axes[idx].set_ylabel(label, fontsize=11)
    axes[idx].set_title(label, fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
    axes[idx].set_xticks(x_pos)
    axes[idx].set_xticklabels([m.capitalize() for m in modes])
    axes[idx].legend(fontsize=9)
    axes[idx].set_ylim([0, 1])
    axes[idx].grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis="y")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(working_dir, "ablation_final_comparison.png"), dpi=300)
plt.close()

# Plot 5: Direct difference visualization
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 6))
for idx, (metric, label) in enumerate(zip(metrics_to_plot, metric_labels)):
    differences = [
        final_results["baseline"][mode][metric]
        - final_results["ablation"][mode][metric]
        for mode in modes
    ]
    colors = ["#2ecc71" if d > 0 else "#e74c3c" for d in differences]

    bars = axes[idx].bar(modes, differences, color=colors, alpha=0.8)
    axes[idx].axhline(y=0, color="black", linestyle="-", linewidth=1)
    axes[idx].set_ylabel(f"Baseline - Ablation", fontsize=11)
    axes[idx].set_title(f"{label} Difference", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold")
    axes[idx].grid(True, alpha=0.3, axis="y")

    for bar in bars:
        height = bar.get_height()
        axes[idx].text(
            bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2.0,
            height,
            f"{height:.3f}",
            ha="center",
            va="bottom" if height > 0 else "top",
            fontsize=10,
        )

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(working_dir, "ablation_differences.png"), dpi=300)
plt.close()

# Save experiment data
np.save(os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), experiment_data)
print(f"\nExperiment data saved to {os.path.join(working_dir, 'experiment_data.npy')}")

print("\n" + "=" * 80)
print("ABLATION STUDY COMPLETE")
print("=" * 80)
print(f"Visualizations saved to: {working_dir}")
print("Generated plots:")
print("  - ablation_appropriate_reliance.png")
print("  - ablation_decision_accuracy.png")
print("  - ablation_agreement_rate.png")
print("  - ablation_final_comparison.png")
print("  - ablation_differences.png")

# Summary of findings
print("\n" + "=" * 80)
print("KEY FINDINGS")
print("=" * 80)
print("\nImpact of removing noise injection (baseline vs ablation):")
for mode in modes:
    ar_diff = (
        final_results["baseline"][mode]["appropriate_reliance"]
        - final_results["ablation"][mode]["appropriate_reliance"]
    )
    da_diff = (
        final_results["baseline"][mode]["decision_accuracy"]
        - final_results["ablation"][mode]["decision_accuracy"]
    )
    print(f"\n{mode.upper()} mode:")
    print(f"  Appropriate Reliance difference: {ar_diff:+.4f}")
    print(f"  Decision Accuracy difference: {da_diff:+.4f}")
    print(
        f"  Interpretation: {'Noise injection helps' if ar_diff > 0 else 'Deterministic performance better'}"
    )
